Consider the example code E = `defaultdict(list).default_factory = None`. What we want is a whitelist file W such that vulture makes the following report:

vulture(E + W) -> no dead code

It is ok to have the following (since it's the reason we need a whitelist file to begin with):

vulture(E) -> dead code

We always need to make sure that whitelist files don't contain unused code, but this is the case in my proposal. 

vulture(W) -> no dead code

I could see this being `env.user` instead of `root`. While `root` would be available to the end-user in most small installations and individual users, it's requesting significantly more privilege than may be required. In an environment where end-users do not have root access, this default would fail saying you don't have permissions to sudo rather than do not have permissions to delete file. So to be usable and not require more privileges by default, we may want to default to `env.user` and have the end-user specify e.g. `storm` or `root` if it didn't match their `env.user` for SSHing.

On the flip side, if we set `env.user` as the default, the first time someone in a small environment with full privileges gets an error they could change `env.user` to be `root` instead of passing `-u` all of the time, which would completely defeat any security.

I think this should be `params["page"]` rather than `params["pageSize"]`.  The `pageSize` parameter in the API determines how many mentions you get per request, while the `page` parameter determines which block of mentions you get back.  (For instance to get 15000 mentions you could make 3 requests each with `pageSize` 5000 and with `page` equal to 0 in the first request, 1 in the second, and 2 in the third.) ```python
```python
{
    'host': 'localhost',
    'port': 12345,
    'primary_slot_name': 'foo-bar',
    'restore_command': 'true',
    'archive_cleanup_command': ''
    'recovery_min_apply_delay': '10min'
}
```

We can assume that this is a standby cluster when either `host` or `restore_command` are defined.

Let me try to outline what happens when we overwrite `self.queryset` and `get_queryset` gets called multiple times.

This is just a simplified pseudo version of what actually happens in DRF:

```python
class BaseView():
  def get_queryset():
    return self.queryset

class MyMixin():
  def get_queryset():
    queryset = super().get_queryset()
    self.queryset = queryset.filter(username='test')
    return self.queryset

class MyView(MyMixin, BaseView):
  queryset = User.objects.all()


view = MyView()
# first get queryset call
queryset = view.get_queryset()
print(queryset.query)
# Output is SELECT * FROM auth_user WHERE username = test

# second get queryset call
queryset = view.get_queryset()
print(queryset.query)
# Output is SELECT * FROM auth_user WHERE username = test AND username = test
```

When `self.queryset` is not overwritten the result of the 2nd call is the same as the first call. In my above example the query is not the same but the result will still be the same. However there might be even example where results might be different when traversing through `ManyToMany` relations and several joins happens.

Hope this explains.

So, here's a fork where `CustomRecurrentLayer` is changed so that it doesn't flatten trailing feature dimensions and can work fine with convolutional input-to-hidden/hidden-to-hidden connections: https://github.com/craffel/nntools/commit/e9f44fc0c6766c2ffd4668b27545a27aa86cde31

e.g.:
```Python
n_batch, n_steps, n_channels, width, height = (2, 3, 4, 5, 6)
n_filters = 7
filter_size = (3, 3)

l_in = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((n_batch, n_steps, n_channels, width, height))
l_in_hid = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(
    lasagne.layers.InputLayer((n_batch, n_channels, width, height)),
    n_filters, filter_size, border_mode='same')
l_hid_hid = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(
    lasagne.layers.InputLayer((n_batch, n_filters, width, height)),
    n_filters, filter_size, border_mode='same')
l_rec = CustomRecurrentLayer(l_in, l_in_hid, l_hid_hid)
out = lasagne.layers.get_output(l_rec)
test_in = np.zeros((n_batch, n_steps, n_channels, width, height), np.float32)
print out.eval({l_in.input_var: test_in}).shape
```
yields `(2, 3, 7, 5, 6)`.  Note that `border_mode` has to be `same`, because otherwise on each pass of `step` the hidden state shrinks! Well, now that you jog my memory, I believe the point of the `charset_static` knob is that if you send a `text/plain` file without specifying a charset, then browsers will assume it's `latin-1`, which seems like a bad default in the age of `utf-8`. So `charset_static` is a way to override this web-wide default for Aspen applications.

`charset_dynamic` is used in the `Content-Type` header (as with `charset_static`), _and_ to encode the body (for static resources the body can be thought of as already having been encoded).
